summary: Julia Evans announces she's open sourcing her nginx playground project, a small tool for testing nginx configurations she built in 2021. She explains why she hesitated to open source it before—she struggles with maintaining open source projects, feels bad about ignoring issues, and worried about messy code. Her new approach is to simply be honest that the project is unmaintained rather than pretending she'll improve it later. She documents the caveats, tests the build instructions, and releases it as-is.
key_points: Open sourcing doesn't require committing to ongoing maintenance—you can release code as unmaintained and still provide value
The belief that you'll improve code 'later' is often a blocker that prevents sharing it at all
Being honest about a project's limitations (security caveats, messy code, no maintenance) is better than pretending you'll fix them
She handed off both her major open source projects (rbspy, rust-bcc) to others who did better, showing self-awareness about her strengths
money_quotes: I'm not very good at open source.
I usually ignore basically all feature requests and most bug reports and then feel bad about it.
In the past I've had some kind of belief that I'm going to improve the problems with my code later.
So instead of pretending I'm going to improve things, I decided to just: tell people in the README that the project is unmaintained.
The README is mostly an extended apology for the developer experience and note that the project is unmaintained. But I did test that the build instructions work!
key_insight: The best way to open source imperfect projects is to stop pretending you'll fix them and instead be upfront about their limitations.
